core_intuitions: Human labeling cannot match RL generation throughput, latency, or consistency; an AI judge closes the feedback loop in milliseconds at a fraction of the cost, which is the entire motivation for CAI/RLAIF.

CAI's two stages attack two bottlenecks: SL-CAI critique-revision turns a harmful draft into clean SFT data, and RLAIF turns AI preference labels into a reward signal, with a written constitution making the judging criterion explicit and human-auditable.
Trusting an AI judge requires debiasing it: swap A/B order to cancel the innate position bias, and multi-vote at temperature to keep only self-consistent labels, discarding ambiguous pairs.

Every self-improvement method shares ONE loop (generate candidates, filter/label by some supervision source, retrain, repeat) and differs only in WHERE the supervision signal comes from: a verifiable reward, the model as its own judge, human gold data, or a weak supervisor.

Rejection-sampling iteration converts sampling-time compute into model capability: each round the greedy pass@1 accuracy climbs toward best-of-k, narrowing the gap; STaR rescues unsolved problems with hint-conditioned rationales and ReST raises the acceptance threshold each round.

Weak-to-strong generalization: a strong student trained on a weak supervisor's noisy labels can exceed the supervisor (the elicitation gap), which is the crux of scalable oversight when models surpass human raters.

existing.name: cai-two-stage-pipeline
existing.issue: Clear and correct in both themes, but the 'Apply constitution principle P_i' box is tight: the small document glyph at the box's left edge collides with the first word 'Apply' of the centered label, and the two title lines sit close to the box border. A slight but real overlap that reads as clutter at the visual center of Stage 1.
existing.improvement: Widen the 'Apply constitution' box (or shrink/move the document glyph up into a corner) so the glyph and the word 'Apply' no longer touch; give the two label lines a touch more horizontal breathing room. No structural change needed - the flow, loop-back arc, and Stage-1-to-Stage-2 hand-off all read well.

existing.name: cai-alignment-data-flywheel
existing.issue: Dense but well-organized: the model-version spine, the RLAIF/QA stages column, the human-vs-AI callout, and the large iterative-upgrade feedback arc all read clearly in both light and dark. Only a hair of overlap where the feedback-arc arrowhead re-enters the seed box near the word 'small' in the subtitle; not worth a structural edit.
existing.improvement: Optional micro-nudge: end the feedback arc a few px lower/left of the seed box so its arrowhead clears the 'small, expensive, high-quality' subtitle. Otherwise leave as is.

new_figures.name: rlaif-judge-debiasing
new_figures.concept: How RLAIF turns a biased AI judge into trustworthy preference labels: position-order swapping plus self-consistency multi-vote filtering.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's central claim is that an AI judge can replace human raters, and the ONLY reason to believe that is the debiasing protocol. Position bias (LLMs favor the first response they read) and label noise are the non-obvious failure modes; the two mitigations map directly to the robust_ai_label code but are invisible in prose. A reader who sees the swap-and-average and vote-and-threshold mechanics understands why AI labels are usable, not just cheap. Neither existing figure touches this.
new_figures.placement: ### The Labeling Protocol (under 'RLAIF: Replacing Human Raters with AI')
new_figures.brief: One comparison example threaded through two stacked mechanism panels. Top strip: a single input card - Prompt, then Response A and Response B side by side - flowing into an 'AI judge' block. PANEL 1 'Position-bias swap': present the SAME pair in two orders, order (A,B) and order (B,A), each producing a raw verdict; annotate the judge with a small tilted balance/scale glyph and the caption 'position bias: the judge leans toward whichever response it reads first'. Show the two orderings combining (average / pair them) into a single de-biased verdict, caption 'swap order, combine to cancel the lean'. PANEL 2 'Self-consistency vote': show n independent judge calls at temperature > 0 producing a short column of verdicts (use small illustrative integers, e.g. n=5 votes reading A, A, B, A, A), a tally box showing 4/5 = 0.8, and a threshold gate 'keep only if consistency >= 0.7'. Two outcomes branch from the gate: KEEP -> emit a labeled (chosen, rejected) pair that flows to a small 'reward model' target; DISCARD -> a greyed-out 'too ambiguous, dropped' pair. Reading order top-to-bottom, left-to-right. Theme-safe: use the accent color for the chosen/kept path and a muted/warn tone for the discarded path; no hardcoded primaries. Keep only small illustrative integers, invent no benchmark numbers. Everything inside the viewBox, no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: self-improvement-signal-map
new_figures.concept: A map of the self-improvement family (RFT, STaR, ReST, self-rewarding, SPIN, weak-to-strong) organized by the single axis that distinguishes them: where the supervision signal comes from.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's second half introduces six named techniques in quick succession and a reader easily drowns in the zoo. The unifying insight is that they all run the same loop and differ only in what fills the 'supervision' slot - a verifiable checker, the model itself, human gold, or a weak model. A single scaffolding figure that pins each method to its supervision source gives the mental index for the entire back half of the chapter, which currently has zero figures. This is the highest-leverage new figure for intuition.
new_figures.placement: ## Rejection Sampling, STaR & ReST (place immediately under the section heading, before the RFT subsection)
new_figures.brief: Center-left: draw the shared backbone loop once, as a compact cycle - 'current policy generates k candidates' -> '[SUPERVISION] filter / label' -> 'retrain (SFT or DPO)' -> back to policy - with the SUPERVISION node visually highlighted as the pivot. To the right, a clean table-like column of six method rows, each row naming exactly what plugs into the SUPERVISION slot and how 'chosen/keep' vs 'rejected/discard' is defined. Rows, ordered top-to-bottom by increasing reliance on the model itself (external ground truth at top, self-supervision at bottom): (1) RFT - supervision = a verifiable reward r(x,y)=1; keep correct completions, drop the rest (a filter). (2) STaR - same verifiable filter PLUS a rescue path: for problems with zero correct samples, reveal the gold answer and have the model rationalize backward to it, adding hint-conditioned examples (small 'hint' rescue arrow). (3) ReST - same filter but with a rising acceptance threshold tau each round (small upward-ratchet glyph, 'bar rises each iteration'). (4) Self-rewarding LM - supervision = the SAME model in judge mode; chosen = highest self-score, rejected = lowest (DPO); note shared weights. (5) SPIN - supervision = human gold as chosen, the model's own current output as rejected. (6) Weak-to-strong - supervision = a weaker model's labels training a stronger student. Give the table two light column headers: 'source of CORRECT / chosen' and 'source of WRONG / rejected or filter'. Keep to six rows, generous line spacing, no wrapped-text collisions. Theme-safe: accent only for the highlighted SUPERVISION pivot and the chosen path; neutrals elsewhere. No invented numbers. Must be fully legible as a static frame.

new_figures.name: rft-capability-ratchet
new_figures.concept: Rejection-sampling iteration converts sampling-time compute into model capability: each round the greedy pass@1 accuracy climbs toward best-of-k, narrowing the gap.
new_figures.rationale: The 'core mechanism' the chapter explicitly names - RFT converts sampling-time compute into capability - is a dynamic that a paragraph and a worked example only gesture at. Showing the filter keeping correct samples, those becoming training data, and the greedy accuracy ratcheting up toward best-of-k across rounds makes the monotonic self-improvement click, and gives STaR's hint and ReST's rising threshold a concrete place to attach. Distinct intuition from the signal map (which is static taxonomy); this one is about WHY iterating works.
new_figures.placement: ### ReST: Reinforced Self-Training (place near the 'Worked example: rejection-sampling pass rates' admonition)
new_figures.brief: Two coupled panels. LEFT 'one round of RFT': a single problem x fans out into k sampled completions (draw ~5 small tokens); a reward-filter gate marks some with a check (correct, kept) and some with an X (wrong, dropped) - e.g. 2 kept, 3 dropped; the kept ones flow into a 'new SFT data' bin, then 'fine-tune -> next policy'. Render the loop-back as a ratchet/gear-up motif to signal the improvement is monotonic (the model can only get more concentrated on correct answers). Attach two small annotations: a STaR 'rescue' arrow on a problem that produced zero checks ('no correct sample? reveal gold answer, rationalize backward'), and a ReST 'threshold tau rises each round' tag on the filter gate. RIGHT 'capability ratchet across rounds': a schematic performance axis (low->high, no numeric ticks) with iterations 0,1,2 on the x-axis; per round plot TWO markers - pass@1 (greedy, lower) and pass@k (best-of-k, higher) - connected by a vertical 'gap' bracket. Across rounds the pass@1 marker climbs toward pass@k and the gap bracket visibly shrinks. Caption the mechanism: 'each round keeps only correct completions -> the policy concentrates probability on correct answers -> greedy accuracy rises toward best-of-k'. Use purely schematic bar/marker heights: do NOT print benchmark percentages; small illustrative round indices only. Theme-safe accent for the kept/correct path and the rising pass@1 marker; muted for dropped samples. All inside the viewBox, clear reading order left-to-right.

new_figures.name: weak-to-strong-generalization
new_figures.concept: A strong student trained on a weak supervisor's noisy labels can exceed the supervisor (the elicitation gap), the crux of scalable oversight.
new_figures.rationale: Weak-to-strong is a full section built on a genuinely counterintuitive result - the student beats its own teacher - that is central to the scalable-oversight frontier the SOTA box flags. A static frame can capture the whole aha (three capability levels, noisy labels flowing up, the student generalizing past them) plus name the interventions that shrink the gap. Nice-to-have rather than must-have because the signal map already places weak-to-strong in the family, but the exceeding-the-supervisor picture is worth its own frame.
new_figures.placement: ## Weak-to-Strong Generalization (immediately under the section heading)
new_figures.brief: A vertical performance axis (low at bottom, high at top), with three horizontal reference levels drawn as bars/lines of increasing height: (1) WEAK SUPERVISOR performance - lowest; (2) STRONG STUDENT trained on the weak labels - lands clearly ABOVE the supervisor but BELOW level (3); (3) STRONG-SUPERVISED CEILING - the performance a strong model reaches under clean/strong supervision. Draw the weak supervisor emitting a stream of labels toward the strong student, with a few labels visibly flipped/wrong (small X marks) to signal noise; then an upward arrow from the student showing it GENERALIZING PAST the noisy signal to land above its teacher - annotate 'the student exceeds its supervisor'. Bracket the vertical distance between level (2) and the ceiling (3) and label it 'elicitation gap'. Tag the gap bracket with the three interventions that shrink it: 'bootstrapping (train a medium model first, then relabel for the strong one)', 'confidence-weighted loss', 'consistency regularization'. Add a framing caption tying it to scalable oversight: 'future models will surpass human raters - can weak supervision still elicit strong capability?'. Use schematic heights only, no invented benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: accent for the strong student and the exceed arrow, muted for the noisy labels and the ceiling reference. Keep labels non-overlapping, everything inside the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is well served on its FIRST half (CAI two-stage pipeline and the alignment data flywheel are both covered by clear, theme-correct existing figures - keep the flywheel, lightly refine a glyph/text collision in the pipeline). The SECOND half - RLAIF's trust-the-judge protocol and the entire self-improvement family (RFT/STaR/ReST/SPIN/self-rewarding/weak-to-strong) - is concept-rich but figure-barren. I propose four new figures, two must-have: (P1) rlaif-judge-debiasing, showing position-swap plus self-consistency voting that makes AI labels trustworthy; (P1) self-improvement-signal-map, a scaffolding taxonomy that pins each self-improvement method to its supervision source and gives the reader a mental index for the whole back half. Then (P2) rft-capability-ratchet, visualizing how iterated rejection sampling converts sampling compute into capability by narrowing the pass@1-vs-best-of-k gap, and (P3) weak-to-strong-generalization, capturing the counterintuitive student-exceeds-supervisor result behind scalable oversight.
